Sales Associate Brand Guidelines Listing Guidelines SOTHEBYSREALTY.CA 24 Listing Guidelines PROPERTY LISTING ON SOTHEBYSREALTY .CA & SOTHEBYSREALTY .COM The details of a property listing are key to attracting the right prospective buyers. Like any successful advertisement, it is your opportunity to showcase your product in its best light. Property listing details must be efficient and informative providing just the right degree of depth. The intent is to communicate a property’s most appealing features, in a clear and timely fashion, while addressing all or most home buyer

questions. ATTEND TO THE DETAILS • Submit all amenities and lifestyle information, include all of the details. • Carefully submit all of the elements needed to ensure your property listing is complete, including: » 10 clear, crisp, high-resolution photographs » Accurate pricing information » A meaningful property headline designed to be attention-grabbing but refined » High definition video » Photo captions should be included along with amenities tags to make sure that your property is displayed on sothebysrealty.ca and affiliate networks. There must be a minimum of 20 characters and no more than 200 characters. • Sotheby’s International Realty Canada or Sotheby’s International Realty Québec may not be abbreviated in any way across any marketing medium (eg: SIRC, SIRQ Sotheby’s, Sotheby’s Intl Rlty Can – not allowed.) • Never make political, religious or other statements that are inappropriate. • Avoid using exclamation points (!), question marks (?), number signs (#) or other symbols. • Avoid terms like “Short Sale, ” “Cheap, ” “Bargain” etc..in the property headline • Never use ALL CAPITAL letters. Use Uppercase/lowercase format • Be mindful of research findings when writing copy; avoid the words associated with low-end or inferior properties like “cozy” and “charming” (which read as old, small) and extreme words like “fantastic, ” which are generally taken to mean there is nothing special to say about the property. • T ry to use complete thoughts in headlines and avoid repeating words (“views, views, views”) or using over- blown phrases or superlatives to describe properties or yourself as these tend to create skepticism. • Any quotes or testimonials regarding a property, development or Sales Associate must come from external sources and be properly attributed. (Approval for use should be obtained in writing.) • Do not use words and terms that are sensitive and legally banned from major real estate web sites such as realtor.ca. • Avoid real estate abbreviation like “EIK” for Eat in Kitchen or “BR” for Bedroom. Write these descriptors out completely in online spaces and other advertising opportunities where space is not limited. • T ell the story of the property you have listed. A robust property description should be a part of every property listing which appears on sothebysrealty.com. For properties like land or lots, tell the story by painting a picture of the property’s potential. • Write your description in a word processing program so that you can spell-check your

description prior to submitting it to your office administrator. • Keep in mind that the customer audience for sothebysrealty.com is global and some references may not make sense to a customer who lives in a different culture and/or country from yours. Sales Associate Brand Guidelines Listing Guidelines SOTHEBYSREALTY.CA 25 Correct: This custom-designed, 3,736 square-foot condominium commands mountain views and overlooks Park City, Utah, from the 10th floor of the St. Regis Deer Valley, a 1,825- acre, ski-in, ski-out resort. The floor plan is designed for entertaining with four en-suite bedrooms, four full baths, a powder room, hot tub, dining room with cathedral ceiling, dine-in kitchen and three stone fireplaces. Finishes and appointments include Jerusalem limestone tile in entryway, Richard Marshall wood floor throughout the living area, Wolf appliances, motorized draperies and Crestron Audio system. Incorrect: Architect Stan Williamson pursued his vision for a MULTIUSE, RESIDENTIAL-VACATION COMPLEX in the Rocky Mountains when he purchased a 15-acre tract outside Park City, Utah, in 1976. He built 20 units, offered five floor plans and customized the finish to each buyer’s preference. All houses in the complex now are part of the Deer Valley Homeowner’s association, which maintains the complex to the highest standards. This unit includes four bedrooms, four bathrooms, dining room, eat-in kitchen, dining room and many vacation friendly amenities. Y ou will be proud to call this condo your winter home!!! HEADLINE The headline must be succinct – 10 words or less – and include the most important aspect of the listing, whether that be a feature of the home itself or its location, history or potential. Do not include the town or city because it already appears in the listing’s location field. However, do include district or neighbourhood information if that detail enhances the listing’s value. The purpose of the headline is to stop prospective buyers and compel them to peruse the photos and details, not to tell the entire story. Please note that on the search results only 30 characters, which is about 3 words, will appear. Longer headlines will be truncated by an ellipsis. Correct: Magnificent Beachfront Living in Victoria Incorrect: The Price is Right on this New-Build Condo Featuring a State- of-the-Art Kitchen, Four Bedrooms and Dine-In Kitchen PROPERTY DESCRIPTION Imagine you have 1 minute to pitch a prospective buyer. What would you say? That’s the challenge of the Overview paragraph. It essentially is your “elevator speech,

” your 1 minute to describe each listing in a way that conveys the most important facts that any buyer would need to consider before purchasing a property. T ell the story of the home. Do not just list the amenities. Features and benefits should be written in the present tense to convey the energy of the moment and make the home come alive. The suggested word count is 200 words or less, though it is highly recommended to include at least 100 words. Sales Associate Brand Guidelines Listing Guidelines SOTHEBYSREALTY.CA 26 WRITING TIPS • Describe special spaces that perhaps you were unable to get a good photo of. • Mention any upgrades and high-end brand name appliances etc. • Discuss the neighbourhood and any great locations nearby. • Avoid derogatory aspects of the property listing and highlight the best points about it. • Recycle bits of copy that can carry from one property to another to help make your property descriptions more robust. • Do not use “RENTAL” in the title for a rental. • A title, or property name, is required for every property listing. • Abbreviations should not be used in property descriptions and copy should be robust. (sq.ft., sq.mt., approx. etc.) • The numbers one through nine should be spelled out as such within a property description. • Local lifestyle insights. • Include any historic or special significance of the home, its neighbourhood or the community. • Highlight the involvement of any notable architects or designers. • Mention prominent past owners/residents. • Past or present notable members of the community. PHOTO CAPTIONS Captions tell the story represented by each image. Captions are written in the present tense to describe the scene as if it were being viewed live. There must be a minimum of 20 characters and no more than 200 characters. Y our caption will appear on both the listing details page and in the gallery. Correct: The ever-changing cycle of nature and flurry of outdoor activities play out day after day through the floor-to-ceiling window walls of the master bedroom. Incorrect: Invite your entire family for a fabulous ski vacation in Utah’s premiere ski destination, where you can sleep 16 in four bedrooms. Three bathrooms means there will be no waiting in the mornings! AREA DESCRIPTION The location paragraph is an opportunity to promote the neighbourhood and surrounding community. This paragraph should include the

top reasons to live there including amenities and services. Consider describing the neighbourhood and what it’s like to walk down the street. The suggested word count is 200 words or less, though it is highly recommended to include at least 100 words. Copy should NOT be copied from another source such as Wikipedia. Sales Associate Brand Guidelines Exclusive Listings SOTHEBYSREALTY.CA 28 Exclusive Listings Public marketing of exclusive listings is prohibited by Canadian Real Estate Association’s (CREA). MLS® LISTING MANDATORY WITHIN THREE DAYS OF PUBLIC MARKETING CREA requires that any residential property publicly marketed by a REALTOR® be placed on the MLS System within three days. Public marketing includes one-to-many communication such as company or broker websites, yard signs, email newsletters, ads, social media, press releases, direct mail, digital marketing, and other public channels. EXCLUSIVE LISTINGS: ONE-TO-ONE MARKETING ONLY Exclusive listings cannot be marketed publicly. Doing so triggers the requirement to list the property on MLS. T o ensure compliance and to protect the legal interests of our agents and brokerage, Sotheby’s International Realty Canada does not display exclusive listings on public-facing platforms including sothebysrealty.ca, sothebysrealty.com, global property syndication, yard signs, email newsletters, ads, social media, press releases, direct mail, digital marketing, or any other public marketing channel. Marketing for exclusive listings is limited to one-to-one communications, such as: • Direct conversations between a listing agent and a potential client or another agent • Individual email communication from a listing agent to a potential client or another agent QUEBEC COMPLIANCE In Quebec, the provincial regulator, OACIQ, enforces mandatory forms and regulations that take precedence over provincial or national association rules, including CREA. Please consult your Managing Broker on current restrictions on marketing exclusive listings. Sales Associate Brand Guidelines Listing Video & Matterport SOTHEBYSREALTY.CA 29 Listing Video & Matterport PROPERTY VIDEO Professional video plays a significant role in successfully marketing a property for sale. The number of exposure opportunities can exceed listings with just photos and text alone. In order for the video to be on sothebysrealty.ca and sothebysrealty.com the following criteria must be met: • Between 1 to 3 minutes in length

that share the unique nature of the property and reflects the brand. • Ultra High-Definition (UHD) and High-Definition (HD) videos in 4K (3840 x 2160 px) and in a 16:9 aspect ratio. When not available, 1080p is the minimum acceptable resolution. • Music track with optional voiceover. • Professionally taken video. • No on-screen logos, graphics, or text. Written or graphic identification is not needed within the video because it will appear on the same page as your listing. • All major file formats (MP4, AVI, MPEG) are accepted – web-friendly mp4 preferred. The file must be less than 1GB in size. Audio • A backing track of royalty-free (right to use copyrighted material or intellectual property without the need to pay royalties or license fees), or licensed music is required. • If narration is added to the video, it must be a clear and crisply recorded voice-over that providing descriptive information in an unobtrusive manner. Avoid regional dialects. Style • We suggest that you hire a professional videographer or use a tripod or stabilizing system to avoid shaky video. • Avoid jump cuts, in which two sequential shots of the same subject are taken from camera positions that vary only slightly. • Do NOT use still imagery. This will avoid duplicating the look and feel of our existing Virtual T ours, which will create an inconsistent feel and fail to take full advantage of the video medium. • Keep the focus on the property, with minimal superfluous on-screen elements. When used sparingly and in a refined, elegant manner, on-screen talent has the ability to add to the quality of the production. experiences that play automatically with no audio. and terrorist financing regime stems from international obligations and standards developed by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). Canada is a member of the FATF and is evaluated for its adherence to the policies of the FATF, including those developed to include the real estate sector. Real estate brokers or sales representatives (the “Agent(s)”) and Sotheby’s International Realty Quebec (the “Brokerage”) must fulfill specific obligations as required by the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act (PCMLTFA) and associated Regulations (the “PCMLTFA Regime”), to help combat money laundering and terrorist activity financing in Canada. Information regarding these obligations can be found in the legislation itself, in FINTRAC’s guidelines, on FINTRAC’s website, and in the information CREA has provided on REALTOR Link® and in WEBForms®, as well as Sotheby’s International Realty Canada’s Agent Dashboard located through a secure log in on the company’s website. FINTRAC operates within the legislative authority of the PCMLTFA Regime with a mandate to prevent, detect, and deter money laundering and terrorist financing activities, while ensuring the protection of the information under its control. FINTRAC assesses and analyzes the data from reports it receives and creates a picture that serves to uncover financial relationships and networks that will assist law enforcement in investigating or prosecuting offences related to money laundering and terrorist financing, as well as threats to the security of Canada; detect trends and patterns related to money laundering and terrorist activity financing risks; uncover vulnerabilities of Canada's financial system; and enhance public awareness of money laundering and terrorist financing matters. The purpose of this manual is to consolidate the basic information that REALTOR® members and Brokerage staff must know to comply with the PCMLTFA Regime and to provide a document of our Brokerage’s policies and procedures; however, you may still need to resort to other resources for more detailed information, indicated as web links throughout this manual and available on FINTRAC’s website . This manual includes information available to comply with regulations effective June 1, 2021. 1. COMPLIANCE POLICY AND PROCEDURE OBLIGATIONS The PCMLTFA Regime requires brokers to establish a compliance program and policies, which must contain several elements. The compliance program and policies must include: i. Appoint a compliance officer responsible for implementing the program and policies; ii. Developing and applying written compliance policies and procedures that are kept up to date and approved by the executive team; iii. Conducting a risk

assessment to assess and document the risk of a money laundering offence or a terrorist activity financing offence occurring in the course of the Brokerage’s activities; iv. Developing and maintaining a written, ongoing compliance training program for employees, Agents or mandataries, and other authorized persons; v. Instituting and documenting a plan for the ongoing compliance training program and delivering the training plan; and vi. Instituting and documenting a plan for a review of the compliance program for the purpose of testing its effectiveness, and carrying out this effectiveness review every two years at a minimum (two -year effectiveness review). All the above elements are covered in this manual. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 5 2. APPOINTMENT OF COMPLIANCE OFFICER The Brokerage’s Compliance Officer is Madeleine Tyber. Ms. Tyber has the necessary authority and access to resources to implement an effective compliance program and make any desired changes; knowledge of the Brokerage’s functions and structure; knowledge of money laundering and terrorist financing activities, vulnerabilities, trends, and typologies common in the real estate industry; and an understanding of real estate industry requirements under the PCMLTFA Regime. The duties of the Compliance Officer are: i. To register with FINTRAC and obtain an ID number, username, and password for reporting purposes ii. To file any necessary reports with FINTRAC relating to suspicious transactions or terrorist property (the Brokerage does not accept cash or virtual currency); iii. To maintain associated records as required by FINTRAC; iv. To implement and administer a compliance regime for money laundering and terrorist property financing reporting; v. To ensure that all employees and Agents receive training and to keep a record indicating when those requiring such training received it; vi. To ensure that all employees and Agents receive ongoing training as required to remain current with legislative changes or changes to the Brokerage’s policies and procedures; vii. To prepare any necessary forms to facilitate reporting by employees or Agents within the Brokerage to the Compliance Officer; viii. To assess the need to review compliance policies and procedures; ix. To stay up to date on FINTRAC information by subscribing to FINTRAC’s mailing list ; and x. For the purposes of ongoing monitoring of business relationships, review the purpose and intended nature of business relationships and oversee risk reassessments of clients. 3. P OLICIES AND PROCEDURES 3.1 R ECORD KEEPING REQUIREMENTS 3.1.1 General Record Keeping Policy The PCMLTFA Regime establishes

that whenever an Agent acts as an agent or mandatary in the purchase or sale of real estate, they are required to create and maintain: 1. Reports (Suspicious Transaction Reports, Terrorist Property Reports, Large Cash Transaction Reports, and Large Virtual Currency Transaction Reports); 2. Large cash transaction records; 3. Large virtual currency transaction records; 4. Receipt of funds records; 5. Information records; and 6. Identification of unrepresented party records. The Brokerage’s policies with respect to record keeping are: i. The Compliance Officer shall develop and implement a filing system for the keeping of forms or records required for compliance. ii. All records prepared for compliance with FINTRAC are the property of the Brokerage and shall be kept in a manner that permits the Brokerage to access them within thirty (30) days of a request from FINTRAC for the Brokerage to provide them. iii. All records, forms, and materials prepared for compliance purposes shall be kept for a period of at least five (5) years from the date of submission. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 6 iv. When completing records about your clients, be as descriptive as possible. 3.1.2 Reports When completing a Suspicious Transaction Report or Terrorist Property Report, you must keep a copy of the report. As the Brokerage does not accept cash or virtual currency, Large Cash Transaction Reports and Large Virtual Currency Transaction Reports are not applicable. More information about reports, including when and how to report a transaction, is available in the Reporting Requirements section of this manual. 3.1.3 Large Cash Transaction Records The Brokerage does not accept cash in any amount on any real estate transaction. 3.1.4 Large Virtual Currency Transaction Records The Brokerage does not accept virtual currency in any amount on any real estate transaction. 3.1.5 Receipt of Funds Records The Brokerage’s policies and procedures with respect to Receipt of Funds Records are: Whenever an Agent receives funds for a purchase or sale transaction, they are required to complete and maintain a Receipt of Funds Record including when bank drafts or certified cheques are issued from a bank account. This is required even if the funds are being held by the listing brokerage. 3.1.5.1 Content Whenever an Agent receives funds in any amount, they are required to complete and maintain a Receipt of Funds Record. You must include on the Receipt of Funds Record: i. the date you receive the funds;

ii. if the amount is received from a person, their name, address, date of birth, and occupation, or in the case of a sole proprietor, the nature of their principal business; iii. if the amount is received from or on behalf of an entity, its name, address, and the nature of its principal business; iv. the amount of funds received and the amount of any part of the funds that is received in cash; v. the method by which the amount is received (for example, in person, by mail, etc.); vi. the type and amount of each fiat currency involved in the receipt; vii. the exchange rates used and their source (if applicable); viii. if an account was affected by the transaction (for example, funds withdrawn from or deposited to an account), include: a. the account number and type of account; and b. the name of each account holder; ix. if other persons are involved in the transaction, their name, address, date of birth, and the nature of their principal business (in the case of a sole proprietor) or occupation; x. if other entities are involved in the transaction, their name, address and the nature of their principal business; xi. every reference number connected to the transaction that is meant to be similar to an account number; and xii. the purpose of the transaction. If the Receipt of Funds Record is in respect of an entity that is a corporation, you must also keep a copy of the part of its official corporate records that contains provisions relating to the power to bind the corporation regarding the Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 7 transaction (for example, the articles of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation that set out the officers duly authorized to sign on behalf of the corporation, such as the president, treasurer, vice-president, comptroller, etc.). The Receipt of Funds Record form, used to record this information, is available on REALTOR Link® and WEBForms®. Agents can also utilize the ReallyTrusted platform to complete a Receipt of Funds Record. It must be accompanied by an Identification Information Record, to indicate the identity of the individual or entity providing the funds. If the individual/entity providing the funds is the same as the client, you don’t need to complete multiple Client Information Records. However, if the individual/entity providing the funds is not the same as the client, the individual/entity

providing the funds will need to be id entified. Follow the steps in the Know Your Client Requirements section of this manual for completing the Identification Information Record and attach it to the Receipt of Funds Record (to avoid confusion, you may indicate that the record concerns the indi vidual/entity providing the funds and is not the client). Note that if multiple accounts are affected, information on all accounts affected needs to be recorded. For example, assuming the buyer’s brokerage transfers funds from their account into the listing brokerage’s account, both accounts are affected by the t ransaction and therefore both numbers are to be recorded on the Receipt of Funds Record. There is no requirement to provide another brokerage with copies of the completed Receipt of Funds Record. 3.1.5.2 Responsibility for Completion Where Agents receive funds from clients represented by an Agent, it is the responsibility of Agent that holds the relationship with the client providing the funds that must keep the record. Where there are two Agents involved in a transaction and the funds are deposited in the listing brokerage’s trust account, the buyer's Agent is responsible for completing the receipt of funds record. However, the buyer’s Agent is not required to include the number and type of the listing brokerage’s trust account or the name of the person or entity that is the holder of that account if, after taking reasonable measures, they are unable to do so. In situations where there is only one Agent involved, the Agent involved in the transaction is obligated to record all specified account information, including trust account information. 3.1.5.3 Exceptions There is no need to complete a Receipt of Funds Record for any amount received from a very large corporation or trust (corporation or trust), a public body (a Canadian provincial or federal department or Crown agency, a municipal body, or a hospital authority), or a subsidiary of those entities, if the financial statements of the subsidiary are consolidated with those of the public body, very large corporation or trust. The funds must originate from the financial entity itself. The mere fact that an individ ual gives an Agent a bank draft or certified cheque drawn on a bank account does not qualify for this exception. A very large corporation or trust is one that has minimum net assets of $75 million CAD on its last audited balance sheet, whose

shares or units are traded on a Canadian stock exchange or on a stock exchange designated under subsection 262(1) of the Income Tax Act, and which operates in a country that is a member of the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). Tip: A Receipt of Funds Record does not need to be completed if the deposit has been paid directly by a buyer to a third party (i.e. a lawyer), as there is no Agent receiving funds. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 8 3.1.6 Information Records Agents must keep an information record on every person or entity for which they act as an agent or mandatary for the purchase or sale of real estate. If the client is a corporation, you must keep a copy of the part of the official corporate records that contains provisions relating to the power to bind the corporation regarding the transaction (for example, the articles of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation that set out the officers duly authorized to sign on behalf of the corporation, such as the president, treasurer, vice-president, comptroller, etc.). If you have multiple clients for a transaction, then an information record must be kept about each client. For example, an Agent representing a couple for a purchase or sale must keep an information record about each person. Information records must include: ● if the client is a person, their name, address, date of birth, and occupation, or in the case of a sole proprietor, the nature of their principal business; or ● if the client is an entity, its name, address, and the nature of its principal business. More details about Information Records are available in the Know Your Client Requirements section of this manual. 3.1.7 Identification of Unrepresented Party Records As an Agent, if you are involved in a real estate transaction with an unrepresented party, and you are acting as an Agent for another party (your client), then you must take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the unrepresented party. If you are not able to verify the unrepresented party's identity after taking reasonable measures, you must keep a record of the measures you took, the date the measures were taken, and why you were unable to verify the party's identity. More details about identifying unrepresented parties are available in the Know Your Client Requirements section of this manual. 3.2 K NOW YOUR

CLIENT REQUIREMENTS 3.2.1 When to Verify the Identity of Persons and Entities Agents must verify client identity when they act as an agent or mandatary in the purchase or sale of real estate. The PCMLTFA Regime requires that Agents verify the identity of clients for large cash transactions, large virtual currency transactions, suspicious transactions, receipt of funds, information records, and unrepresented parties. As the Brokerage does not accept cash or virtual currency, the requirement to identify clients for large cash or virtual currency transactions is not applicable. 3.2.2 Suspicious Transactions You must take reasonable measures to verify the identity of every person or entity that conducts or attempts to conduct a suspicious transaction, regardless of the transaction amount, before sending a Suspicious Transaction Report (STR). This includes transactions that would normally be exempt from client identification requirements. You do not have to take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the person or entity that conducts or attempts to conduct a suspicious transaction if you have already verified the identity of the person or entity and have no doubts about the identification information or believe that verifying the identity of the person or entity would inform them that you are submitting an STR. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 9 More information about STRs is available in the Reporting Requirements section of this Manual. 3.2.3 Receipt of Funds You must verify the identity of a person from whom you receive funds in any amount at the time the transaction takes place. You must also verify the identity of a corporation or other entity from which you receive funds in any amount, or on whose behalf the transaction is conducted, within 30 days after the day on which the transaction is conducted. There is no need to complete a Receipt of Funds Record for a very large corporation or trust, a public body, or their subsidiaries where financial statements are consolidated, as outlined in the Record Keeping Requirements section of this manual. Reminder: If an Agent receives funds from a client represented by another Agent, then it is the broker or sales representative that holds the relationship with the client providing the funds that must identify the client. Please refer to the Record Keeping Requirements section of this manual for details on completing the Receipt of Funds Record. 3.2.4 Information Records You must verify the identity of every person or

entity for which an information record is kept. It is the Brokerage’s policy that Agents verify a client’s identity when a listing is accepted or when an offer is made, and no later than when an offer is accepted. Please refer to the Methods to Verify the Identity of Persons and Entities section of this manual for details on completing Identification Information Records. 3.2.5 Unrepresented Parties When there are unrepresented parties in a transaction, all Agents representing a party to the transaction must take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the person or entity that is unrepresented at the time of the transaction. This includes sellers who have listed their properties using a brokerage, but only as a “mere posting” and are not receiving any additional services from that brokerage. If unable to ascertain that party’s identity, Agents should indicate in the Identification Information Record that the required information is unavailable and describe the measures taken to obtain the information. Tip: If the unrepresented party is physically present, you may simply ask for ID, and if the unrepresented party refuses to provide it, document that they were asked on a given date and refused. 3.2.6 Methods to Verify the Identity of Persons and Entities The information that is required to verify a client’s identity will vary depending on whether the client is an individual (a live human being), corporation, or other entity. Verifying identity is a foundational element of the PCMLTFA Regime and a key component of the Brokerage’s relationship with clients. It helps you to know your clients and to understand and assess any risk that may be associated to their transactions or activities. 3.2.6.1 Identifying Individuals To verify the identity of an individual, Agents must complete all required and applicable fields in the Individual Identification Information Record (available through REALTOR Link® and WEBForms®), including but not limited to the client’s name, address, date of birth, and the client’s principal business or occupation. You must be as descriptive as possible regarding the business or occupation of the client. Where possible include a client’s specific title and the name of their employer, or if self-employed, the name of their business and what field Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 10 they are in, including what work they perform. Record information that clearly describes it, rather than using a general term. For example, in the case of a consultant,

the occupation recorded should reflect the area of consulting, such as “information technology consultant” or “consulting forester”. As another example, in the case of a professional, the occupation should reflect the nature of the work, such as “petroleum engineer” or “family physician”. Agents can use the following methods to verify the identity of individuals: 1. Government-issued photo identification method - A client will need to produce an authentic, valid, and current provincial, territorial, or federal piece of ID with a unique identifier number and photo while in your presence for this method. If any of the information obtained is not valid or current, or you are unable to verify the document’s authenticity, you must seek assistance from your Managing Broker or use another method. To use this method, you must also record the date of verification of information, the identifying document’s unique identifying number, the identifying document’s expiry date, the identifying document’s jurisdiction, and the type of identifying document used on the Individual Identification Information Record. Please refer to FINTRAC’s guidance for examples of acceptable photo identification documents. 2. Credit file method – You may verify the identity of a person by referring to information in their credit file, but only if the credit file contains valid and current information, is from a Canadian credit bureau, has been in existence for at least three years, contains information from at least two source (i.e. multiple tradelines), and matches the client’s name, address, and date of birth. You must conduct this search yourself at the time of identity verification. If any of the information obtained is not valid or current, you must search a different credit file or use another method. To use this method, you must record the date you consulted or searched the credit file, the name of the Canadian Credit Bureau or third- party vendor, and the person’s credit file number on the Individual Identification Information Record. 3. Dual-process method – You may verify the identity of a person by referring to information from a reliable source that includes any two of the following options: a. The person’s name and address; b. The person’s name and date of birth; or c. The person’s name and confirmation they have a deposit or prepaid payment product account, or a credit card or other loan account with a financial entity. You must confirm the information provided under all options, which must be

valid and current and come from two different and reliable sources. Reliable sources are trusted, reputable, and well -known. If there are any inconsistences or if you are unable to determine the information is valid and current, you must use a different method. To use this method, you must also record the date of verification of information, the names of the two reliable sources, the type of information referred to, and any numbers associated with the information on the Individual Identification Information Record. 4. Affiliate or member method – You may verify the identity of a person by confirming that an affiliate of the Brokerage has previously verified the person’s identity. Under this method, the affiliate must have previously verified the person’s identity using the government -issued photo identification, credit file, or dual-process methods. The only affiliate that can be used for this method are other Sotheby’s International Realty Canada brokerages. To use this method, you must also record the affiliate’s name, the date of verification of information, the method used for identification by the affiliate, and the information the affiliate used to complete the identification on the Individual Identification Information Record. 5. Reliance method – You may verify the identity of a person by relying on measures previously taken by a Sotheby’s International Realty affiliate brokerage, but only if the affiliate has similar record keeping, Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 11 identity verification, and compliance program policies in place, under supervision of a competent authority under that state’s legislation. Please discuss with your Managing Broker to confirm the foreign affiliate’s practices and policies are similar enoug h to the Brokerage’s. Under this method, the affiliate must have previously verified the person’s identity using the government -issued photo identification, credit file, or dual-process methods. To use this method, you must also record the affiliate’s name, the date of verification of information, the method used for identification by the affiliate, and the information the affiliate used to complete the identification on the Individual Identification Information Record. Additionally, to use this method you must have a written agreement with the affiliate that requires them to provide you, upon request, with all information referred to in the identity verification process. Please refer to this guidance from FINTRAC if you have any questions, or discuss with your Compliance Officer or Managing Broker. 3.2.6.2 Identifying Corporations and Other Entities To verify the identity

of a corporation or other entity, Agents must complete all required and applicable fields in the Corporation/Entity Identification Information Record (available through REALTOR Link® and WEBForms®). Agents must use the confirmation of existence method to confirm the existence of the corporation or entity, as the Brokerage does not permit the use of the reliance method or the simplified identification method to identify corporations or entities. FINTRAC also requires the person conducting the transaction to be identified, using the Individual Identification Information Record. Entities To verify the identity of an entity using the confirmation of existence method, you may refer to a partnership agreement, articles of association, or the most recent version of any other record that confirms its existence and contains its name and address. Corporations To verify the identity of a corporation using the confirmation of existence method, you may refer to a certificate of incorporation, a record that must be filed annually under provincial securities legislation, or the most recent version of any other record that confirms the corporation's existence and contains its name and address and the names of its directors, such as a certificate of active corporate status, the corporation's published annual report signed by an audit firm, or a letter or notice of assessment for the corporation from a municipal, provincial, territorial, or federal government. If the corporation is a securities dealer, you do not need to confirm the names of its directors. All records referred to must be authentic, valid, and current. The documents used to verify an entity or corporation’s existence do not have to be in hard copy; an electronic document may be used if it is a public record and includes the registration number, such as Corporation Canada's federal corporation’s database, which is accessible from Industry Canada’s website . To use this method, you must keep a copy of any record referred to, in addition to the source and type of record if using an electronic document. Tip: Don’t forget the receipt of funds requirement still applies to corporations and other entities. You need to ID the person giving you the funds as well, if not received by someone who has already been identified. Please refer to this guidance from FINTRAC if you have any questions, or discuss with your Compliance Officer or Managing Broker. 3.2.6.3 Identification through an Agent or Mandatary Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 12 If

Agents are not able to personally verify a client’s identity, they can use an agent or mandatary to carry out the verification on their behalf, in accordance with the government -issued photo identification method, the credit file method, or the dual-process method. To use an agent or mandatary to verify the identity of a person you must: a. Have the Identification Mandatary/Agent Agreement, available on REALTOR Link® and WEBForms®, in place with the agent or mandatary before you use them; b. Obtain, as soon as feasible, all of the information that the agent or mandatary referred to in order to verify the person's identity, and the information that the agent or mandatory confirmed as being that of the person; and c. Be satisfied that: a. the information that the agent or mandatary confirmed as being that of the person is valid and current, and b. the person's identity was verified using the government-issued photo identification method, the credit file method, or the dual-process method, or, if the person's identity was verified prior to June 1, 2021, using the methods in accordance with the PCMLTF A Regime in place at the time. You may rely on the measures that were previously taken by an agent or mandatary to verify the person's identity, if the agent or mandatary was: a. acting in their own capacity at the time, regardless of whether they were required to use the methods in accordance with the PCMLTFA Regime or not; or b. acting as an agent or mandatary under a written agreement or arrangement that was entered into with another reporting entity under the PCMLTFA Regime, for the purposes of verifying a person's identity using either the government-issued photo identification method, the credit file method, or the dual -process method, or if the measures were taken prior to June 1, 2021, using the methods in accordance with the PCMLTFA Regime that were in place at the time. When you verify the identity of a person by using an agent or mandatary, you must keep a record of the person's name, the written agreement or arrangement with the agent or mandatary for verifying a person's identity, all the information the agent or mandatary used to verify the identity of the person, and the information that the agent or mandatary confirmed as being that of the person (this includes, as applicable, information that is required to be kept

in the record for the method used). Note: It is the Agent’s responsibility to meet their client identification requirements even when using an agent or mandatary to verify the identity of a person on their behalf, or when relying on measures previously taken by an agent or mandatary to verify a person's identity. Please refer to this guidance from FINTRAC if you have any questions, or discuss with your Compliance Officer or Managing Broker. 3.2.6.4 Identifying Clients who are not Physically Present At this time, the Brokerage utilizes ReallyTrusted technologies to authenticate government -issued photo identification, so identification of clients not physically present can only occur via ReallyTrusted. The ReallyTrusted app is compliant with FINTRAC’s guidance, as their technology asks a person to scan their government-issued identification document and then compares the features against known characteristics to be satisfied that it is an authentic, valid, and current document as issued by the competent authority. Additionally, ReallyTrusted’s technology asks the person being identified to take a “selfie” and applies facial recognition Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 13 technology to compare the features of the “selfie” to the government-issued photo identification document, as well as comparing the name on the government -issued photo identification document with the name provided by the person. 3.3 BUSINESS RELATIONSHIP REQUIREMENTS FINTRAC defines a business relationship as a relationship established between a reporting entity and a client to conduct financial transactions or provide services related to financial transactions. Agents enter into a business relationship with a client the first time that they are required to verify a client’s identity. Once you enter into a business relationship, you are required to keep a record of the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship. This is accomplished using section D.1 of the applicable Identification Information Record. Business relationships last five years from the day of the last transaction that required verification of the client’s identity. 3.3.1 EXCEPTIONS A business relationship is not formed where an Agent is not required to verify the identity of a client due to an exception for identification verification under the PCMLTFA Regime. For example, no business relationship is formed when conducting a transaction for a public body, very large corporation, or a subsidiary of either of those whose financial statements are consolidated. 3.4 ONGOING MONITORING REQUIREMENTS Ongoing monitoring applies once Agents enter into business relationships with clients. For clarity, you are

in a business relationship with every client whose identity you have verified. The purpose of ongoing monitoring under the PCMLTFA Regime is to detect suspicious transactions that require reporting; keep client identification information, beneficial ownership information, and the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship record up to date; reassess the level of risk associated client transactions and activities; and determine whether transactions or activities are consistent with client information obtained and your risk assessment of the client. The Brokerage’s policies and procedures with respect to ongoing monitoring are: ● Ongoing monitoring of individual clients shall include, at a minimum, asking clients if there has been a change to their name, address, or principal business or occupation o If there has been a change, the Agent must record it on the Individual Identification Information Record ● Ongoing monitoring of corporate clients shall include, at a minimum, asking if there has been a change to the corporation’s name, address, or the names of its directors o If there has been a change, the Agent must record it on the Corporate Identification Information Record ● Ongoing monitoring of entity clients shall include, at a minimum, asking if there has been a change to the entity’s name, address, or principal place of business/operation o If there has been a change, the Agent must record it on the Entity Identification Information Record ● Agents must also indicate any changes to the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship using section D.1 of the Identification Information Record or ReallyTrusted’s ongoing monitoring feature ● The Compliance Officer shall review Identification Information Records and ReallyTrusted records and add additional information to section D.1 or to the ReallyTrusted ongoing monitoring record if they feel an Agent has inadequately described the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 14 ● Agents shall keep all correspondence with the client on file, as noted in Section D.2.2 of the Identification Information Record ● Where another transaction is conducted with a client who has an existing business relationship with an Agent, the Compliance Officer and the Agent will review the Identification Information Record to determine whether all the client’s transactions or activities are consistent with the information obtained and the initial risk assessment 3.4.1.1 When to Conduct Ongoing Monitoring The PCMLTFA Regime requires that ongoing monitoring is periodically conducted after entering into

a business relationship with a client. The frequency at which ongoing monitoring is conducted is dependent on the risk level assigned to the client, indicated on section C of the applicable Identification Information Record. The Brokerage’s policies and procedures with respect to the frequency of ongoing monitoring are: ● Low-risk client relationships must be monitored at least every two years ● Medium-risk client relationships must be monitored at least every year ● High-risk client relationships must be monitored at least every six months The requirement to conduct ongoing monitoring ends when the business relationship ends, which is five years from the day of the last transaction that required verification of the client’s identity. 3.4.1.2 Enhanced Ongoing Monitoring for High-Risk Clients The PCMLTFA Regime requires that clients identified as posing a high risk in section C of the applicable Identification Information Record be subject to additional, enhanced ongoing monitoring requirements. Following the identification of a high-risk client, the Compliance Officer, Managing Broker(s), and the Agent involved will discuss which enhanced ongoing monitoring methods will be utilized. Enhanced ongoing monitoring of high-risk clients may include: ● Indicating the measures taken on section D.2.3 of the Identification Information Record ● Reviewing transactions of high-risk clients on an approved schedule that involves management sign-off ● Developing reports and reviewing reports for high-risk transactions more frequently ● Flagging any activities that deviate from expectations or otherwise raise concerns ● Setting limits or parameters on accounts or transactions that would trigger early warning signals and require mandatory review ● Reviewing transactions more frequently against suspicious transaction indicators relevant to business relationships ● Obtaining additional information about high-risk clients, through public databases or internet searches o This may include volume of assets, source of wealth, transaction reasons or intentions, etc. ● Identifying patterns of transactions that need further examination ● Increased monitoring of transactions of higher-risk client groups and locations ● Use of more stringent thresholds for ascertaining identification ● Gathering additional documents, data, or information ● Taking additional steps to verify any documents obtained ● Establishing transaction limits ● Increasing awareness of high-risk activities and transactions ● Increasing internal controls of high‐risk business relationships ● Obtaining the approval of senior management for any new transactions with a high-risk client Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 15 The requirement to conduct enhanced ongoing monitoring ends when the business relationship ends, which is five years from the

day of the last transaction that required verification of the client’s identity, or when the client is no longer considered high risk based on the Agent’s risk assessment. Note that the requirement for ongoing monitoring still exists until the end of the business relationship. 3.4.1.3 Ongoing Monitoring Records The PCMLTFA Regime requires the Brokerage to keep records on the processes in place for ongoing monitoring and enhanced ongoing monitoring for high-risk clients, documented in this manual. Agents must keep records of the measures taken and the information obtained during ongoing monitoring processes. The Brokerage’s policies and procedures with respect to recording information related to ongoing monitoring are: ● All Agents shall complete section D.1 of every Identification Information Record used to record verification of a client’s identity, or if verification was completed through ReallyTrusted, Agents shall conduct ongoing monitoring through ReallyTrusted’s platform ● If an Agent discovers that client information has changed, it shall be recorded on the Identification Information Record or on the ReallyTrusted ongoing monitoring record ● All Agents shall keep relevant correspondence and records with the client on file to help ensure that the client’s activities match the other information recorded ● Agents must notify their Compliance Officer if they are working with a client that may be high risk 3.5 B ENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP REQUIREMENTS Concealment of beneficial ownership information is a technique used in money laundering and terrorist activity financing schemes. Identifying beneficial ownership removes the anonymity of the individuals behind the transactions and account activities, which is a key component of the PCMLTFA Regime. FINTRAC defines beneficial owners as individuals who directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of a corporation or entity, or the trustees, known beneficiaries, and settlors in the case of a trust. For widely held or publicly traded trusts, the beneficial owners are the trustees and all persons who own or control, directly or indirectly, 25% or more of the units of the trust. Beneficial owners cannot be other corporations, trusts, or other entities; they must be the individuals who are the owners or controllers of the entity. It is important to consider and review the names found on official documentation to confirm the accuracy of the beneficial ownership information. It may be necessary to search through many layers of information to confirm who are the beneficial owners, as the names found on official documentation may not always reflect

the actual beneficial owners. No beneficial ownership information is required when verification of an entity or corporation isn’t required. The Brokerage’s policies and procedures with respect to beneficial ownership information are: ● In all cases, Agents must obtain information establishing the ownership, control, and structure of the entity or corporation ● Reasonable measures taken to confirm the accuracy of beneficial ownership information must align with your assessment of the entity or corporation's risk for money laundering or terrorist activity financing offences ● Reasonable measures taken to confirm the accuracy of beneficial ownership information of entities with a complex business structure must go further, to ensure that you are able to understand and confirm the accuracy of beneficial ownership information obtained Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 16 3.5.1.1 When and How to Obtain and Confirm the Accuracy of Beneficial Ownership Information Agents must obtain beneficial ownership information when they verify the identity of an entity or corporation. To obtain beneficial ownership information, Agents can have the entity or corporation provide it verbally or in writing, or search for publicly available information. Reasonable measures to obtain beneficial ownership information may include: ● Asking the client ● Conducting open-source searches ● Retrieving information readily available, including electronic and paper formats ● Consulting commercially available information Agents must take reasonable measures to confirm the accuracy of the beneficial ownership information when it is first obtained and throughout the course of ongoing monitoring of business relationships. Reasonable measures taken to confirm the accuracy of the beneficial ownership information cannot be the same as the measures used to obtain the information. Reasonable measures to confirm the accuracy of beneficial ownership information may include referring to: ● For trusts, the trust deed ● Minute books ● Securities registers ● shareholders registers ● Articles of incorporation ● Annual returns ● Certificates of corporate status ● Shareholder agreements ● Partnership agreements ● Board of directors' meeting records of decisions ● Having clients sign a document to confirm the accuracy of beneficial ownership information obtained o In this case, one document can be used for both by having the client sign the document ● Asking the client to provide supporting official documentation ● Conducting an open-source search ● Consulting commercially available information 3.5.1.2 Types of Beneficial Ownership Information Required Corporations – the names of all directors of the corporation and the names and addresses of all persons who

directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of the shares of the corporation Trusts – the names and addresses of all trustees and all known beneficiaries and settlors of the trust Widely held or publicly traded trusts – the names of all trustees of the trust and the names and addresses of all persons who directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of the units of the trust Entities other than corporations or trusts – the names and addresses of all persons who directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of the entity For non- profit organizations, Agents must also determine if the entity is a charity registered with the Canada Revenue Agency under the Income Tax Act or an organization, other than a corporation or entity, that solicits charitable donations from the public. As a best practice, Agents should also confirm whether a not -for-profit organization is a charity registered with the Canada Revenue Agency by cons ulting the charities listing on Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 17 the Canada Revenue Agency website. 3.5.1.3 Beneficial Ownership Information Records Agents must keep records of the beneficial ownership information obtained and the measures taken to confirm the accuracy of that information. The Beneficial Ownership Record, available on REALTOR Link® and WEBForms, should be used to document information obtained and the measures used to confirm the accuracy of the information. The Brokerage’s policies and procedures with respect to recording information related to ongoing monitoring are: ● For corporations, Agents must record: o the names of all directors of the corporation; o the names and addresses of all persons who directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of the shares of the corporation; and o information establishing the ownership, control and structure of the corporation ● For trusts, Agents must record: o the names and addresses of all trustees, known beneficiaries and known settlors of the trust; and o information establishing the ownership, control and structure of the trust ● For widely held or publicly traded trusts, Agents must record: o the names of all trustees of the trust; o the names and addresses of all persons who directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of the units of the trust; and o information establishing the ownership, control and structure of the trust ● For entities other than corporations or trusts,

Agents must record: o the names and addresses of all persons who directly or indirectly own or control 25% or more of the entity; and o information establishing the ownership, control and structure of the entity ● For non-profit organizations, Agents must record whether the entity is: o a charity registered with the Canada Revenue Agency under the Income Tax Act; or o an organization, other than a registered charity, that solicits charitable donations from the public ● If Agents are unable to obtain beneficial ownership information, keep it updated in the course of ongoing monitoring of business relationships, or confirm its accuracy at any time, they must take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the entity's chief executive officer or of the person who performs that function and apply special measures for high-risk clients, including enhanced ongoing monitoring ● If Agents determine that there is no individual who directly or indirectly owns or controls 25% or more of a corporation, a widely held or publicly traded trust, or an entity other than a corporation or trust, they must keep a record of the measures taken and the information obtained to reach that conclusion All beneficial ownership records must be kept for a period of at least five years from the date the last business transaction was conducted with the client. 3.6 T HIRD PARTY DETERMINATION REQUIREMENTS FINTRAC defines a third party as a person or entity that instructs another person or entity to conduct a transaction or activity on their behalf. As such, the third party is the instructing party to the transaction or activity and is also understood to be the "on behalf of" party. Agents are required to take reasonable measures to determine whether a third party is involved in a real estate transaction. The obligation to take reasonable measures to make a third-party determination applies when you are required to keep an Identification Information Record, at the time you create the record. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 18 It is the Brokerage’s policy that any information about third parties be documented using section B of the applicable Identification Information Record. As indicated elsewhere in this manual, Identification Information Records must be kept for a period of at least five years from the date the record was created. 3.6.1 REASONABLE MEASURES TO OBTAIN INFORMATION ABOUT A THIRD PARTY If you determine that there is

a third party involved, you must take reasonable measures to obtain the following information about the third party, based on the type of party: People — their name, address, telephone number, date of birth, and occupation, or in the case of a sole proprietor, the nature of their principal business Corporation or other entity — its name, address, telephone number, the nature of its principal business, its registration or incorporation number, and the jurisdiction (province or state) and country of issue of that number For all types of third parties, you must take reasonable measures to obtain information about the relationship between the third party and the person or entity that the information record is kept on. Example: The relationship between the person or entity and the third party can be an accountant, broker, customer, employee, friend, or relative. If you are not able to make a third-party determination but have reasonable grounds to suspect that a third party is involved, you must keep a record that describes the reason(s) why you suspect that the person or entity is acting on behalf of a third part y. The record should indicate whether the client has indicated they are acting on their behalf only. It is the Brokerage’s policy that Agents inform the Compliance Officer directly when they suspect a third party is involved but are unable to confirm. 3.7 P OLITICALLY EXPOSED PERSONS AND HEADS OF INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS REQUIREMENTS Recent amendments to the PCMLTFA Regime introduced requirements for Agents to determine whether clients are politically exposed persons (PEPs) and heads of international organizations (HIOs). FINTRAC defines as domestic PEP as a person who currently holds, or has held within the last 5 years, a specific office or position in or on behalf of the Canadian federal government, a Canadian provincial (or territorial) government, or a Canadian municipal government (including Governor General, lieutenant governor or head of government; member of the Senate or House of Commons or member of a legislature; deputy minister or equivalent rank; ambassador, or attaché or counsellor of an ambassador; military officer with a rank of general or above; president of a corporation that is wholly owned directly by Her Majesty in right of Canada or a province; head of a government agency; judge of an appellate court in a province, the Federal Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada; leader or president of a

political party represented in a legislature; or mayor, reeve or other similar chief officer of a municipal or local government). FINTRAC defines a foreign PEP as a person who holds or has held one of the following offices or positions in or on behalf of a foreign state: head of state or head of government; member of the executive council of government or member of a legislature; deputy minister or equivalent rank; ambassador, or attaché or counsellor of an ambassador; military officer with a rank of general or above; president of a state-owned company or a state- owned bank; head of a government agency; judge of a supreme court, constitutional court or other court of last resort; or leader or president of a political party represented in a legislature. These persons are foreign PEPs regardless of citizenship, residence status, or birthplace. Once you’ve determined a client is a foreign PEP, they remain one forever. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 19 FINTRAC defines an HIO as a person who currently holds or has held within the last 5 years the specific office or position of head of an international organization and the international organization that they head or were head of is either: an international organization established by the governments of states; an institution of an organization established by the governments of states; or an international sports organization. Institutions established by international organizations do not need to operate internationally. International organizations are set up by the governments of multiple member countries, have activities in several countries, are bound by a formal agreement among member countries, have their own legal status, and are an entity distinct from member countries. The HIO is the primary person leading the organization, often as president or CEO. A person ceases to be an HIO 5 years after they are no longer the head of the organization or institution. Tip: Looking at how an organization is established will help you determine whether it is an international organization – if it was established by a formally signed agreement between the governments of at least two countries, it is likely an international organization, meaning its head is likely an HIO. The PCMLTFA Regime considers certain family members of PEPs and HIOs to be subject to the same mitigation of risks as PEPS and HIOs. Family members including spouses or common-law partners, biological or adoptive children, parents, in-laws,

and siblings of PEPs and HIOs, once determined to be so, are family members of domestic PEPs or HIOs until five years after the domestic PEP or HIO has left office, which applies even after the death of a domestic PEP or HIO. Once you’ve determined that a person is a family member of a PEP or HIO, they remain one forever, regardless of the death of the PEP or HIO, and there is no need to make the determination again. Often, ex-spouses or partners continue to have access to the PEP or HIO’s funds even after divorce or separation, so the ex-spouse or partner of a foreign PEP is considered a family member of a foreign PEP forever, and ex-spouses or partners of domestic PEPs or HIOs are considered family members of the PEP or HIO as long as they remain a PEP or HIO. Stepchildren, stepsiblings, nieces, and nephews are not considered family members of PEPs or HIOs. FINTRAC defines close associates of a PEP or HIO as those who are connected via personal or business reasons. Once you determine a person is a close associate of a PEP or HIO, they remain a close associate until the connection is lost. Examples include, but are not limited to: ● Business partners, or beneficial owners or controllers of businesses ● Romantic relationships ● Involvement in financial transactions ● Prominent members of the same political party or union ● Shared board memberships ● Close carrying out of charitable works ● Being jointly listed on policies 3.7.1 WHEN TO MAKE A PEP OR HIO DETERMINATION Agents must take reasonable measures to make a business relationship related PEP, HIO, or family member of a PEP or HIO, or close associate of foreign PEP determination when you: 1. Enter into a business relationship (i.e. when you represent a client in a transaction, creating the need to verify the client’s identity) 2. Conduct periodic monitoring of your business relationships 3. Detect a fact about your existing business relationships that indicates a PEP or HIO connection You do not have to make a PEP or HIO determination if you have already determined that a person is a foreign Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 20 PEP or family member of a foreign PEP. Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 21 3.7.2 HOW TO MAKE A PEP OR HIO DETERMINATION: REASONABLE MEASURES The reasonable

measures Agents should perform in determining whether an individual is a PEP/HIO include: ● Asking the individual ● Conducting a Google search of the individual The simplest approach is asking the individual. 3.7.3 DETECTING A FACT ABOUT A PEP OR HIO FINTRAC defines “detecting a fact” as discovering information (proactively or not) about a person that could lead you to make a PEP or HIO determination, or update information about a known PEP or HIO. You detect a fact when you discover PEP or HIO related information about a person that a non-account-based business relationship with you, outside of your periodic review of existing clients. The information must be a fact that constitutes reasonable grounds to suspect that a person is a PEP or HIO, or a family member or close associate of a PEP or HIO. You might detect a fact that would require further action based on information obtained from an existing client, monitoring efforts you may already have in place, knowledge of domestic and world events, or a search run against an open source or third-party database. While a name match is a fact, it is not necessarily a fact that constitutes reasonable grounds to suspect that an existing client is a PEP, HIO, or family member/close associate of a PEP or HIO. As a best practice, you could apply additional criteria (for example, address, date of birth, age, transaction activities, etc.) to a name match, to meet the reasonable grounds to suspect threshold. If you detect information related to a PEP or HIO determination, you must act on the information, as indicated in the next section. 3.7.4 MEASURES TO TAKE AFTER MAKING A DETERMINATION When you enter into a business relationship with, or detect a fact about a PEP or HIO, or family member/close associate of a PEP or HIO, you have 30 days after the day on which you enter into the business relationship or detect a fact, to take reasonable measures to establish the source of a person's wealth, if applicable. Once you’ve determined that someone is a foreign PEP or family member/close associate of a foreign PEP, you must: ● Take reasonable measures to establish the person’s source of wealth ● Consider the client to be high-risk, and thus subject to enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship, additional identification verification measures, and any other measures appropriate to mitigate the risks identified Once

you’ve determined that someone is a domestic PEP or HIO, or family member of a domestic PEP/HIO, and upon conducting your risk assessment you consider there to be a high risk of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence being committed, you must: ● Take reasonable measures to establish the person’s source of wealth ● Implement enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship, additional identification verification measures, and any other measures appropriate to mitigate the risks identified Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 22 When you detect a fact about an existing business relationship and determine that a person is a close associate of a domestic PEP or HIO, and consider there to be a high risk of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence being committed based on your risk assessment, you must: ● Take reasonable measures to establish the person’s source of wealth ● Implement enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship, additional identification verification measures, and any other measures appropriate to mitigate the risks identified Reasonable measures to establish the source of a person’s wealth can include asking them or referring to open- source information available about the person. If the transaction activity is inconsistent with the information you’ve obtained about the source of the person’s wealth, it is Brokerage policy that you follow up with the client for clarification. If the information remains inconsistent, or you are not satisfied with their response and have reasonable grounds to suspect the transaction is related to the commission or attempted commission of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence, you must file a suspicious transaction report. 3.7.5 PEP/HIO RECORD REQUIREMENTS When you enter into a business relationship, conduct periodic monitoring of business relationships, or detect a fact about an existing business relationship, and determine that the person is a PEP or HIO, or a family member/close associate of one of a PEP or HIO, you must keep a record of the: ● Office or position and the name of the organization or institution of the PEP or HIO ● Date of the determination ● Source of the person's wealth, if known Agents must keep PEP and HIO business relationship records for at least five years after the day on which they were created. 3.8 M INISTERIAL DIRECTIVES The Minister of Finance issues ministerial directives and transaction restrictions to protect Canada's financial system from being used

for money laundering and terrorist financing purposes. The Brokerage is subscribed to FINTRAC’s email list and regularly monitors the FINTRAC website to ensure awareness of all ministerial directives applicable to the real estate sector. The directives currently in force at the time of writing are: ● December 9, 2017: Democratic People’s Republic of Korea (DPRK) ● February 24, 2024: Russia Agents are required to treat all transactions originating from, or destined to, North Korea and Russia as high risk and inform the Compliance Officer immediately. 4. REPORTING REQUIREMENTS The Brokerage and Agents are obligated to file two types of reports with FINTRAC: Suspicious or attempted suspicious transaction reports and terrorist property reports. Large cash transaction reports and large virtual currency transaction reports are not applicable, as the Brokerage does not accept cash or virtual currency for real estate transactions. FINTRAC has obligations when it comes to handling information it receives in reports. These obligations specify Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 23 that FINTRAC is independent from law enforcement and other agencies to which FINTRAC is authorized to disclose information; provide for criminal penalties for any unauthorized use or disclosure of the personal information under FINTRAC's control; and speci fy that the Privacy Act applies to FINTRAC. In summary, FINTRAC is required to ensure that personal information under its control is protected from unauthorized disclosure. Real estate is an industry particularly vulnerable to money laundering and terrorist activity financing. The large amounts paid for real estate make it an attractive option for laundering proceeds of crime. Illegitimate actors or criminals may seek to pay a mortgage down with illegitimate funds, turn illegal proceeds into legitimate equity in real estate, or by making payments from the proceeds of crime. The Brokerage requires that all Agents and employees read this section of the manual to ensure they are familiar with indicators and red flags of money laundering and terrorist financing activities. 4.1 Suspicious Transaction Reports (STR) A suspicious transaction report (STR) is the report that must be submitted to FINTRAC if an Agent or the Brokerage has reasonable grounds to suspect that a real estate transaction or attempted real estate transaction is related to the commission or attempted commission of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence. The STR is an important report for FINTRAC, as it allows FINTRAC to review additional descriptive details surrounding a transaction, such as nicknames,

secondary names, beneficial ownership information, IP addresses, additional account numbers, email addresses, details of purchases or e-transfers, locations, relationships, and background information, which FINTRAC uses in its analysis and production of financial intelligence disclosures. To submit an STR to FINTRAC, you will need to ensure that you have completed the measures that enable you to establish there are reasonable grounds to suspect the transaction is related to the commission of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence, including: ● Screening for and identifying suspicious transactions ● Assessing the facts and context surrounding the suspicious transaction ● Linking money laundering or terrorist activity financing indicators to your assessment of the facts and context ● Explaining your grounds for suspicion in an STR, where you articulate how the facts, context, and money laundering or terrorist activity financing indicators allowed you to reach your reasonable grounds for suspicion For the purposes of an STR, FINTRAC employs the following definitions: Fact � An event, action, occurrence, or element that exists or is known to have happened or existed — it cannot be an opinion. For example, facts about a transaction could include the date, time, location, amount, or type. Facts could also include account details, particular business lines, the client's financial history, or information about the person or entity (for example, that the person has been convicted of a designated offence or is the subject of a production order, or that an entity is being investigated for fraud or any other indictable offence). Context � Information that clarifies the circumstances or explains a situation or transaction. This type of information is essential to differentiate between what may be suspicious and what may be reasonable in each scenario. Context of a transaction can be observed or understood through: ● General awareness of the events occurring in a person or entity's business environment or community ● Your knowledge of the typical financial activities found within the real estate industry ● Regular know your client activities, like verifying the identity of persons and entities, their occupation or Sotheby’s International Realty Canada FINTRAC Compliance Manual 24 business, how they generate their wealth, their typical or expected transactional behaviours, discussed earlier in this manual ● Information obtained through the application of your risk assessment ● Illustrative client details, such as the background, behaviour, and actions of your client A transaction may not appear suspicious in and of itself.

However, a review of additional contextual elements surrounding the transaction may create suspicion. Conversely, the context of a particular transaction, which may have seemed unusual or suspicious from the onset, could lead you to reassess your client's current and past transactions and conclude that they are reasonable in that circumstance. Your suspicion of money laundering or terrorist activity financing will likely materialize from your assessment of multiple elements, including transactions, facts, context, and any other related information. When these elements are viewed together, they create a picture that will either support or negate your suspicion of the commission of a money laundering or terrorist activity financing offence.
